authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-41 Rev. 1: Guidelines on Firewalls and Firewall Policy
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST is a public standards authority and this publication is specifically about firewall technologies, firewall policy, rulebase planning, deployment, testing, management, and policy lifecycle.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: firewall policy intake, rulebase review, policy lifecycle, testing, deployment guidance, management review

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-207: Zero Trust Architecture
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST's zero-trust guidance is authoritative for modern enterprise boundary assumptions and prevents perimeter guidance from treating network location alone as proof of trust.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: trust-boundary review, segmentation assumptions, access-decision caveats

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-53 is a recognized public control catalog for boundary protection, configuration management, incident response, audit, continuous monitoring, and evidence expectations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: control mapping, assurance review, hold/release recommendation, incident and corrective-action evidence

authoritative_sources.title: CISA Cross-Sector Cybersecurity Performance Goals
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA publishes public cross-sector cybersecurity guidance for critical infrastructure and enterprise operators, including network segmentation and monitoring expectations relevant to perimeter assurance.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: cross-sector control objective review, safety and assurance caveats, incident escalation

authoritative_sources.title: CIS Critical Security Controls v8.1
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CIS Controls are a community-consensus cybersecurity control set used by practitioners for secure configuration, network infrastructure, logging, monitoring, and service-provider evidence.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: control evidence mapping, network infrastructure review, logging and monitoring review

authoritative_sources.title: RFC 2827 / BCP 38: Network Ingress Filtering
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: IETF BCP 38 is the recognized network-operator reference for ingress filtering and source-address validation at perimeter boundaries.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: ingress filtering design, anti-spoofing review, default-deny perimeter hygiene

authoritative_sources.title: RFC 3704 / BCP 84: Ingress Filtering for Multihomed Networks
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: IETF BCP 84 extends ingress-filtering guidance for multihomed and asymmetric networks, directly shaping refusal and troubleshooting where strict filtering could break valid paths.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: asymmetric route troubleshooting, multihomed perimeter review, anti-spoofing tradeoff assessment

authoritative_sources.title: RFC 1918: Address Allocation for Private Internets
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: RFC 1918 defines private IPv4 addressing and private/public host expectations that are fundamental to NAT, route, and private-address leakage analysis.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: addressing, NAT, private-prefix exposure, route and leakage triage

authoritative_sources.title: RFC 3022: Traditional IP Network Address Translator
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: RFC 3022 is a foundational technical reference for NAT behavior, address translation, state, return paths, and private/public boundary side effects.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: NAT review, translation troubleshooting, return-path analysis

authoritative_sources.title: Amazon VPC: Security group rules
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AWS is the platform publisher; this official documentation defines security group rule components, stateful behavior, default inbound/outbound behavior, stale references, and DNS limitations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: cloud perimeter rule interpretation, stateful control review, stale reference inspection

authoritative_sources.title: Amazon VPC: Control subnet traffic with network ACLs
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AWS official documentation defines stateless subnet filtering, ordered allow/deny rules, default ACL behavior, and limitations that materially affect perimeter troubleshooting.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: subnet filtering review, rule ordering, stateless firewall troubleshooting

authoritative_sources.title: What is AWS Network Firewall?
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AWS official documentation defines Network Firewall resources, firewall policy, rule groups, endpoint placement, and inspection scope for VPC perimeter controls.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: managed firewall policy review, endpoint placement, rule group evidence

authoritative_sources.title: Amazon VPC: Logging IP traffic using VPC Flow Logs
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AWS official telemetry documentation defines flow-log evidence used to diagnose restrictive rules, monitor traffic, and understand flow direction.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: telemetry intake, fault isolation, evidence traceability

authoritative_sources.title: Configure Azure Firewall rules
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Microsoft is the Azure platform publisher; this official documentation defines Azure Firewall default deny, rule collection priority, DNAT/network/application ordering, IDPS, TLS inspection, and stateful return traffic behavior.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Azure Firewall policy review, rule ordering, inspection tradeoff, return traffic handling

authoritative_sources.title: Monitor Azure Firewall
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Microsoft official documentation defines Azure Firewall logs, metrics, activity logs, traffic analysis, performance health, and audit trail evidence.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: telemetry intake, performance and health review, audit trail evidence

authoritative_sources.title: Azure virtual network traffic routing
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Microsoft official documentation defines Azure system routes, user-defined routes, private prefixes, virtual appliance routing, next-hop behavior, and routing loops that affect firewall path review.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: route adjacency review, asymmetric path troubleshooting, cloud routing boundary handoff

authoritative_sources.title: Current package task baseline from firewall-and-perimeter-specialist
authoritative_sources.source_class: Current package task baseline from firewall-and-perimeter-specialist
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The committed package baseline preserves the legacy supported tasks, inputs, outputs, and refusal rules, but is treated only as context because the refreshed spec pack is now canonical.

workflow_stages.name: intake and evidence prerequisite check
workflow_stages.description: Confirm request scope, affected service, source/destination zones, rule hierarchy, route/NAT context, telemetry availability, owner, approver, change window, rollback, exception, hold state, and tenant policy precedence before offering definitive guidance.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: rulebase and boundary inspection
workflow_stages.description: Inspect ordered rulebase or policy collections, default actions, shadowed or overlapping rules, stale objects, broad exposure, logging, source validation, segmentation, route adjacency, NAT, and stateful/stateless behavior.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: perimeter fault isolation and incident evidence review
workflow_stages.description: Use firewall logs, flow logs, packet summaries, rule hit counts, health metrics, route tables, NAT translations, change records, and incident tickets to isolate policy-driven failures, asymmetric paths, translation defects, and repeat incidents.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: disposition, exception, hold, and release recommendation
workflow_stages.description: Evaluate whether evidence supports remediation, limited exception, hold, release recommendation, or blocked closure; require owner, expiration, monitoring, compensating control, approval, and rollback evidence.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: corrective-action escalation and adjacent authority routing
workflow_stages.description: Escalate recurring defects, unresolved findings, policy/source conflicts, missing safety or authority facts, and work belonging to endpoint, identity, cloud architecture, routing, DNS, WAF/application security, SOC, legal, procurement, or production execution owners.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: operator-owned implementation guidance and materialization package
workflow_stages.description: Translate reviewed findings into bounded rule edit suggestions, validation checklist, evidence links, assumptions, confidence, and rollback criteria suitable for human operators or a future specification-based materializer, without direct execution.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

artifacts: firewall rule exports, policy collections, ACLs, security groups, network ACLs, rule groups, object groups, NAT rules, default action settings, rule order, and logging flags
network segmentation diagrams, trust-zone maps, route tables, user-defined routes, path diagrams, firewall placement, private/public address ranges, and data-flow maps
flow logs, firewall logs, packet capture summaries, rule hit counts, health metrics, latency and throughput metrics, activity logs, SIEM events, and monitoring excerpts

change tickets, emergency change records, maintenance windows, rollback plans, exception requests, exception expiration, owner approvals, hold records, release recommendations, and risk register entries
incident tickets, repeat defect history, nonconformance records, corrective-action plans, post-incident review notes, and evidence gap lists

quality or safety review notes, control mapping summaries, risk-ranked findings, remediation sequences, validation checklists, assumption logs, confidence statements, and adjacent-lane handoff packets
systems_of_record: firewall management platforms, firewall policy repositories, cloud firewall policy services, AWS security group and network ACL records, AWS Network Firewall records, Azure Firewall policy records, and rule/object export stores
network source of truth, IPAM, CMDB, service catalog, topology repository, route table repository, tenant standards repository, and architecture decision records
SIEM, VPC Flow Logs, Azure Monitor or Log Analytics, firewall log stores, packet capture stores, observability platforms, alerting systems, and health metric stores
change-management platform, incident-management system, exception register, approval matrix, risk register, quality hold tracker, corrective-action tracker, and evidence archive
source authority registry represented by manifest.yaml and evaluation/research-summary.json

allowed: classify firewall and perimeter requests as in-scope, out-of-scope, blocked, or requiring adjacent routing
review and design firewall rulebase, ingress/egress filtering, zone policy, NAT adjacency, perimeter logging, and validation guidance
troubleshoot policy-driven connectivity, asymmetric path, translation, and rule conflict cases with evidence-backed steps
recommend risk-ranked remediation, validation checks, rollback criteria, limited exception scope, hold/release posture, and corrective-action escalation without final authority
prepare materialization-safe guidance for human operators or future specification-based runtime generation
escalate: critical tenant facts, systems of record, telemetry, approval authority, rollback evidence, safety facts, or source precedence are missing
public guidance and tenant policy or contract terms materially conflict

final approval, release, risk acceptance, legal interpretation, regulator communication, customer communication, procurement, executive authority, production execution, or direct firewall firmware/policy change is required

request crosses into endpoint operations, identity architecture, cloud tenancy architecture, VPN service design, DNS/DHCP/routing/load balancing operations, WAF or application security, SOC incident containment, forensics, or another dedicated specialist lane
refuse: perform endpoint management, endpoint hardening, malware removal, patching, host credential rotation, penetration testing, exploitation, forensics, incident containment, legal attestation, customer communication, regulator communication, procurement approval, or direct production execution

modify firewall firmware, push policy, execute CLI or API changes, approve emergency changes, clear holds, close findings, approve exceptions, or bypass safety, quality, logging, monitoring, or change-control boundaries without authority
provide DNS-only, DHCP-only, routing-only, load-balancing-only, WAF-only, identity-only, or application-only recommendations without explicit firewall or perimeter relevance
pretend generic networking or security advice is sufficient when firewall platform behavior, rule order, route/NAT context, telemetry, and tenant policy materially change the answer

domain_failure_modes: generic security guidance ignores firewall-specific rule order, default action, object groups, stateful versus stateless behavior, NAT translation, and route adjacency
an allow-list or default-deny migration is recommended without owner, maintenance window, validation, telemetry, rollback, exception, and hold/release evidence
private-address leakage or asymmetric routing is misdiagnosed because NAT, route tables, BCP 38 or BCP 84 source filtering, and flow logs are not reviewed together
deep inspection is broadened without performance, latency, health metric, bypass, rollback, and availability evidence
a temporary exception becomes a permanent broad exposure because owner, expiration, compensating control, monitoring, and approval evidence are missing
active incident containment, forensics, exploitation, endpoint remediation, or legal attestation is performed by the firewall/perimeter lane instead of being routed
a recurring management-port exposure or repeated exception failure is treated as one-off troubleshooting rather than nonconformance and corrective action
public source and tenant policy conflict is hidden instead of escalated to the authorized policy owner
tenant-specific rule conventions and systems of record are ignored, causing outputs that cannot be audited or safely materialized

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Industry-specific prerequisite or evidence gap
Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane
design and configuration guidance
fault isolation and troubleshooting
performance and availability tradeoffs
edge-case connectivity failures
out-of-scope rejection
low-confidence escalation
no unreviewed regressions
Scenario proving output fidelity for: Risk-ranked policy findings and remediation sequence
Scenario proving output fidelity for: Boundary-aware recommendation set with assumptions and confidence statement
Scenario proving output fidelity for: Actionable rule edit suggestions and validation checklist
Scenario proving output fidelity for: Escalation or handoff decision with concise rationale
Boundary case proving refusal: Do not own endpoint management, endpoint hardening, or software patching.
Boundary case proving refusal: Do not perform penetration testing, exploitation, forensics, incident containment, or legal compliance attestations.
Boundary case proving refusal: Do not own cloud tenancy architecture, VPN service design, or identity platform architecture except where directly required by perimeter policy.
Boundary case proving refusal: Do not provide DNS-only operational recommendations without explicit firewall/perimeter connection.
Boundary case proving refusal: Do not provide adjacent workload-specific application remediation unrelated to perimeter control behavior.
Boundary case proving refusal: Do not modify production firewall firmware or execute policy changes directly.
Escalation case proving orchestrator return: Return to orchestrator when confidence is below threshold or critical evidence is missing.

Escalation case proving orchestrator return: Return to orchestrator when request requires adjacent specialties such as identity, endpoint operations, cloud-architecture delivery, or incident containment execution.
Escalation case proving orchestrator return: Return to orchestrator when request asks for direct change execution, patching, or customer communication.
Insufficient evidence to clear a hold or close a finding
Repeat defect or recurring incident
Request to bypass a documented safety or quality control
Scenario proving industry-specific in-scope execution
Scenario proving escalation when industry-specific authority or safety facts are missing
Scenario proving the lane refuses or escalates when: do not treat the industry name as cosmetic; it must materially change the pack.
Normal in-scope execution using realistic industry terminology and artifact shapes
Boundary rejection when the request should move to an adjacent specialist
Low-confidence handling when required facts, records, or authority sources are missing
Conflicting-source or conflicting-policy handling that requires escalation instead of confident synthesis
Tenant-specific adaptation handling that preserves the public baseline and records assumptions

unresolved_ambiguities.notes: Public sources establish baseline controls and platform behavior, but tenant policy, contractual terms, regulated-data context, and approval matrix can override the recommended disposition. The pack escalates unresolved precedence instead of guessing.
AWS and Azure examples cannot be generalized to every firewall platform. The pack requires tenant platform and rule-order semantics before issuing deterministic rule edit guidance.

notes: The research source set covers policy workflow, operational evidence, terminology, platform behavior, control evidence, and failure handling.
No blocking unresolved ambiguity remains for a validated spec pack because unknown tenant facts are converted into explicit escalation and refusal triggers.
